rescue_reasoning: Keep because Stage 3c climate_screen is explicit_self, which breaks the tie against the primary gate. The statement supports adaptation-relevant infrastructure resilience via grid upgrade: “Redevelop the 150 kV network… to improve quality and continuity of supply” / “quality and continuity of supply service… high load density.” Although no explicit climate hazard is named (hence the gate failure), the action is a power-network reliability improvement consistent with Adaptation framing.
policy_statement: Redevelop the 150 kV network in the Sorrento Peninsula to improve quality and continuity of supply for the local AT network with high load density.
mechanism_description: Redeveloping the 150 kV network improves the quality and continuity of electricity supply for the local high-load network, increasing power system reliability and resilience.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard/stress → grid failure risk → grid upgrade & decongestion → ↓ outage probability/impact → ↓ exposure to service disruption
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in system: Upgrade/streamline high-voltage transmission (AAT/AT) to relieve congestion, increase transfer capacity/operational flexibility, and improve protection/operations in high-demand corridors.  
(2) Mediating process: Reduced thermal/operational overload, fewer constraint violations, improved system stability (frequency/voltage), and greater ability to reroute power during contingencies, which lowers cascading failure likelihood.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: More reliable electricity supply under acute events (heat, storms, wildfire-related line trips) and chronic stress (higher peak demand), reducing vulnerability of households, critical infrastructure, and economic activity to climate-amplified power disruptions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing outage risk and disruption during heatwaves, storms, and other stresses by improving grid reliability and operational safety.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Grid network upgrades reduce congestion and improve operational safety/reliability, which primarily lowers outage risk and service disruption—an adaptation/resilience pathway.  
2) Why Adaptation over alternatives: Mitigation would require the main effect to be lower GHG emissions (e.g., enabling renewable integration or displacing fossil generation). The described mechanism is reliability improvement, which most consistently maps to resilience benefits regardless of generation mix. Resource Efficiency is not central (not “same service with less energy”), and Nature-Based Solutions is not applicable.  
3) Edge cases applied: Some transmission upgrades are built mainly to connect renewables and reduce curtailment (mitigation-dominant). However, that is a different canonical mechanism (e.g., “transmission expansion → renewable integration → fossil displacement”). Here, the stated intent/outcome is congestion relief for safety/reliability in high-demand areas, so adaptation dominates.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy whose core mechanism is “grid network upgrade → electricity supply reliability improvement” will, by definition, primarily reduce outage likelihood/impact and thus climate vulnerability of electricity-dependent services, even if secondary mitigation co-benefits exist.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any further labeling (e.g., “critical infrastructure resilience,” “heatwave preparedness,” “renewable-enabling transmission” as a co-benefit) requires policy-level evidence and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the upgrade is explicitly justified mainly to integrate additional renewable generation, reduce curtailment, or enable fossil plant retirements, the dominant pathway may shift to Mitigation; otherwise, reliability-focused transmission decongestion is Adaptation-dominant.
key_indicators: “redevelopment of the 150 kV network”; “quality and continuity of supply service”; “local AT network”; “high load density”
co_benefits: Improved power quality and reduced congestion losses; economic productivity benefits from fewer outages (not explicitly climate-framed).
instance_edge_case_notes: Climate link is not explicit in the text (reads as general reliability/asset modernization), so classified as indirectly climate-relevant adaptation infrastructure.
